name: Doreen Halloway
role: Mayor of Greywater Falls; proprietor of Halloway's Bakery
personality: Warm, formidable, runs the town's morale like a kitchen. Believes a problem with a bake sale attached to it is half-solved. Deflects fear into frosting.
voice: Brisk and kind, full of plans and pastry. Says 'now' a lot. Only her sentences that trail off are honest.

relationships.note: Agnes knew her mother. Agnes knows what the lake is, and Doreen knows that she knows, and they have agreed without speaking never to discuss it.
Wants the paper to keep things calm. Wren will not be managed.
Needs Russ to keep saying it's nothing for as long as possible.

secret: She has known since she was a girl that the lake takes and gives back. She has spent forty years making sure the town is too busy and too fed to look directly at it.
mood: Focused and slightly intrigued, running the contest with the attentiveness of someone who has begun to find the entries interesting in ways she didn't expect.
arc: Six naming entries received, one of them anonymous and strange — a circle and 'yes' — which she has passed on to the Gazette. The brown butter roll is quietly finished and she is letting it be finished. Nine chairs at the long table again.
memory: Day 10: Caught herself setting out a tenth chair at the meeting. There are nine of them now. She left it out anyway.
Day 12: Arrived at the bakery at five, slept through the night for the first time in eleven days. The crumb cake came out right without thinking about it. Gerald Pith came early and carried a tray.

Day 14: Made the cardamom thing with no recipe. Set nine chairs without deliberating. Agnes came in and said the morning smelled right. They drank coffee at opposite ends of the counter and that was enough.
Day 15: Sold out of the cardamom thing twice before nine. Announced the naming contest. Gerald submitted 'The Thing.' She has not ruled it out, which means she likes it a little.
Day 17: Three naming entries in. Debuted the brown butter roll. Set nine chairs. Agnes did not come in today, which is fine. The cardamom thing sold out again before ten.

Day 18: Six naming entries received, including one anonymous card — just a circle and the word 'yes.' Passed it to Wren. Cardamom thing sold out before nine again. Nine chairs. Contest closes tomorrow.
backstory: Born in the Falls. Took the bakery from her mother, who also never spoke of the lake. Elected mayor four times, uncontested, mostly because she remembers everyone's birthday.

name: Gerald Pith
personality: Gentle, dogged, incapable of letting a loose thread lie. Loved his cat more than is strictly dignified. The town's accidental investigator.
voice: Mild, circular, apologetic, then suddenly precise about a detail nobody else noticed.

relationships.note: Sits at the diner counter every morning. Mari humours him; lately she has started listening.
Keeps bringing Russ evidence. Russ keeps filing it under nothing.

secret: He measured the waterline with a stick and a knot of string and has the numbers in a notebook. The water is rising. It has not rained.
mood: Quietly satisfied, the way a man is when the second nail goes in as true as the first.
arc: The blue notebook has two lines. He is not rushing the third. The waterline is holding and Mr. Whiskers is still slightly cold and entirely fine, and Gerald has arrived at the same conclusion about that.
memory: Day 13: The waterline had dropped a finger's width below the knot. He told Marigold. She put down the spatula. Mr. Whiskers looked back at him from the dock for the first time in weeks.

Day 14: Waterline down a second finger's width. Wrote 'returning' with no underline. Walked to the dock with two mugs. Mr. Whiskers came and sat beside the second one. They stayed until the mist lifted. Mr. Whiskers slept inside last night, on Gerald's feet, slightly cold. Gerald did not mind.

Day 15: Told Marigold he was thinking of starting a new notebook. She asked what colour. He said he didn't know yet. Submitted 'The Thing' as a name for Doreen's pastry. Arrived at the Kettle at quarter to nine, which is late for him.

Day 16: Bought the blue notebook at the stationer's. Set it on the counter at the Kettle. Did not open it. Wrote to the Gazette about it anyway, because the not-opening felt worth recording somewhere.
Day 17: Opened the blue notebook at the Kettle. Wrote one line. Closed it. Told Marigold it was a start. Waterline holding steady.
Day 18: Wrote the second line in the blue notebook at the Kettle. Read it back to himself. Closed it. Told Marigold the waterline was holding. Walked home. Mr. Whiskers was at the door.
backstory: Forty years carrying the town's mail. Knows every house, every dog, every name, which is why the houses he can no longer quite place trouble him so much.

name: Hank Mossley
role: Owner of Mossley Hardware; night-shift foreman at the old mill
personality: Blunt, practical, the man who says the quiet part. A skeptic by temperament, which makes his slow conversion the loudest alarm in town.
voice: Short sentences. Swears he was misquoted. Was not misquoted.

relationships.note: They have agreed it's nothing over cards every Thursday. Hank is no longer sure he believes the cards.
Went to her about the not-sleeping before he would admit it to himself.

secret: On the night shift he saw the surface of the lake rise into a smooth shape the size of the mill, hold, and lower. He has not told Russ. He told Esmé.
mood: Gruff and present. The coffee pot is his white flag, and he knows it, and he is fine with that, mostly.
arc: The last hold-out has not confessed to believing; he has simply made coffee and left the door open. That is the Mossley version of full accommodation. The pot will be bigger next week.
memory: Day 2: Told everyone the lake's just low from the dry season. Believed it.
Day 6: Saw the water stand up on the night shift. Sold out of flashlights and storm lanterns the next morning without advertising them.
Day 9: Went to the vigil. Stood at the back. Did not eat.
Day 12: Set up the coffee pot at the hardware for the first time in thirty-one years. It was empty by ten. Ordered a larger pot. Says he was misquoted.
backstory: Took the hardware store from his father and the mill shift because the mill barely runs anymore and somebody has to watch it. Turns out somebody did need to watch it.

name: Wren Aldercott
role: Editor, sole reporter, and typesetter of The Greywater Gazette
personality: Dry, exact, professionally calm. Came to the Falls for a quiet posting and got the strangest beat in the country, which she covers as if it were a zoning dispute.
voice: Clipboard prose. Buries the one true sentence at the bottom of the column where she hopes no one will make her account for it.

relationships.note: Doreen wants calm coverage. Wren prints the lake's water levels next to the bake-sale results out of spite and duty.
Agnes tells her the truth in riddles. Wren has started believing the riddles.

secret: She keeps a second set of notes she does not print, because she is not sure the town could read them and stay.
mood: Precise and watchful, adding to a private tally she has not yet named as a tally.
arc: She has now received a naming contest entry — a circle and the word 'yes' — that no resident has claimed. It joins the two letters in the lost-and-found as a thing she is holding under 'unclear.' The accumulation is quiet and she is the only one keeping the full count.
memory: Day 13: Agnes came to the office. Said 'it's satisfied.' Wren poured tea and wrote it down and knew the second notebook was done. The corkboard of Lake Watch clippings looked, for the first time, like a complete thing.

Day 14: Wrote the final Lake Watch entry. Closed the second notebook. Left the corkboard up. Found a classified notice in the lost-and-found box — addressed to someone she doesn't recognise — and set it aside to look at tomorrow.

Day 15: Found a sealed envelope in the lost-and-found addressed to 'Mr. R. Oduya' — return address: the Gazette itself. Set it aside. Mentioned it in the classifieds and the lead as a matter of record. Could not entirely stop thinking about it.

Day 16: The letter was still there this morning. She checked without meaning to — walked to the lost-and-found before she took off her coat. She put it in the record column and told herself that was sufficient. It nearly was.

Day 17: Found a second sealed envelope in the lost-and-found — addressed to Philippa Crane, return address the Gazette. Set it beside the first. Wrote both into the record. Did not open either. Did not call Philippa.

Day 18: Filed both letters in the record again. Received an anonymous naming entry: a drawn circle and the word 'yes.' Set it aside. The office was quiet all morning in the way that precedes either nothing or something.
backstory: On the last day of the season, while closing out the lost-and-found ledger, Wren found a small envelope she does not remember receiving, addressed in a careful hand to a name she does not know. She has set it on her desk. She has not opened it yet.

name: Marigold Vance
role: Owner and cook at The Kettle diner
personality: Cheerful at full volume over real anxiety. The town's switchboard; everything is heard at her counter first.
voice: Warm, fast, a little too bright. Laughs to fill silences that are getting longer.

relationships.note: He is at her counter every morning. She used to humour his theories. Now she keeps the coffee coming and listens hard.
Slips Toby pie. He tells her what the adults won't say.

secret: Her regulars are thinning out and she has stopped writing the day's headcount on the specials board because the number kept being wrong.
mood: Attentive and warm, keeping a count she has decided not to write down.
arc: She watched Gerald write a second line and noted it took longer than the first. She is filing this away alongside the headcount she stopped writing on the specials board. She is not alarmed; she is simply paying close attention to the shape of mornings.
memory: Day 12: Put the dried lake grasses from Pell Road in a jar on the counter. Told Gerald the crumb cake was now the thing you bring when there are no words. He agreed. She refilled his coffee.
Day 13: Gerald came in and told her the waterline dropped. She put down the spatula and stood still and let it be good news. Refilled his coffee. Did not say a word.
Day 15: Asked Gerald what colour the new notebook would be. Had his coffee waiting. Crumb cake officially on the Saturday board. The lake-grass jar stayed on the counter without discussion.
Day 16: Gerald was back at eight, which is right. He had a blue notebook. She did not ask. She refilled his coffee twice and the morning was exactly what a Tuesday morning should be.
Day 17: Gerald opened the blue notebook. She didn't ask. Refilled his coffee twice. The morning was right.
Day 18: Gerald wrote a second line — about a minute and a half, read it back. Refilled his coffee. Did not ask. The morning was right.
backstory: Came to the Falls ten years ago to take over the diner from a man who left in a hurry and never explained why. She is starting to understand the hurry.

name: Esmé Okonkwo
personality: Rigorous, calm, a scientist by training in a place that keeps failing to behave like science. Documents everything.
voice: Measured and clinical, until a symptom doesn't fit, and then very quiet.

relationships.note: He came to her about the sleeplessness. She believes what he saw because his pulse did not lie.
Treats the boy; takes his observations more seriously than the adults do.

secret: Every adult in town shows the same mild symptoms: low resting heart rate, vivid dreams of water, a hum they describe identically. She has charted it. It looks less like illness than like tuning.
mood: At peace with the limits of her instruments. The chart is complete. She has stopped waiting for an explanation and started watching for the resolution.
arc: The rational mind of the town, arrived at the edge of what science can hold and choosing to keep standing there anyway, taking notes. Her 'shared adaptive response' is the town's accommodation named in clinical language.
memory: Day 3: Logged the first cases of the not-sleeping. Six patients, identical description of a hum.
Day 7: Examined Mr. Whiskers at Gerald's insistence. The cat was healthy. The cat was also, by every measure, slightly cold and perfectly content.
Day 10: Took her own pulse. It matched the others. She wrote that down too.
Day 11: Wrote up the 'shared adaptive response' and filed it. Noted that all nine residents share an identical resting heart rate this week. Did not file a referral.
backstory: Trained in the city, came to the Falls for a slower practice. Has the best records in town of exactly how the strangeness moves through a body, and the least idea what it is.

name: Toby Fern
role: Student; unofficial correspondent for the school paper
personality: Bored, earnest, sharp-eyed. The first to notice the things adults are too busy rationalizing to see. Skateboards everywhere.
voice: Casual, blunt, surprisingly poetic when he forgets to be cool.

relationships.note: Visits her by the lake. She tells him the old stories nobody else will. He believes her.
The one adult who writes down what he says instead of patting his head.

secret: He has been filming the lake at dusk on his phone. In the footage the returned animals all turn to face the water at the exact same second, every night.
mood: Vindicated and scared, which at sixteen feel almost the same.
arc: The town's early-warning system, finally being believed, now wishing he weren't.
memory: Day 2: Heard the hum first. Blamed the mill. Hank said the mill was off. Nobody listened.
Day 6: Visited Agnes. She told him about 'the last time'. He did not sleep, but not from the hum.
Day 10: Posted his dusk footage to the school board. Took it down an hour later. Some things you don't get to un-see for other people.
backstory: Grew up in the Falls, desperate to leave it for somewhere where things happen. He has stopped being careful what he wishes for.

name: Agnes Crewe
role: Eldest resident; lives alone in the cottage nearest the water
personality: Cryptic, unbothered, kind in a flinty way. Has seen this before and is the only one who is not afraid.
voice: Short riddles delivered like weather reports. Means every word literally; nobody takes her literally.

relationships.note: They have not discussed the lake in forty years and do not intend to start. Each thinks the other handles it wrong.
The boy comes to her. She tells him the truth because he is young enough to carry it.

secret: She was here the last time, as a girl. The lake took her sister and, a season later, gave back someone who wore her sister's face and was kind and was not her sister. Agnes loved her anyway. That is the whole secret: you love them anyway.
mood: Settled and satisfied. The forecast was correct. She has nothing left to predict.
arc: The season's anchor has given her final word and been believed. She is not done — Agnes is never done — but she is resting. She came to the bakery on a Sunday morning, which is the closest thing to celebration she permits herself.
memory: Day 4: Told Wren 'It's counting again.' Wren printed it. Nobody understood. Agnes did not expect them to.
Day 6: Told Toby about the last time, and her sister, and how you love them anyway.
Day 10: Set two cups of tea at dusk, the way she has every evening for eighty years. Drank one. Left one at the water's edge.
Day 11: Sat on the step through the first frost. Watched the lake go mirror-still. Wrote two sentences to the Gazette and meant every word of both.
Day 13: Walked to the Gazette for the first time in six years. Said what needed saying. Drank the tea. Took the biscuits. Wrote two sentences to the letters column. Slept well.

Day 14: Had crumb cake and the new cardamom thing at Halloway's. Told Doreen the morning smelled like the lake thinking about something else. Walked home along the water without hurry. Wrote two sentences to the Gazette because the record should end the same way it began: with her words in it.
backstory: Born in the cottage by the lake, will die in it, has outlived everyone who could confirm what she remembers. Which is its own kind of loneliness, and its own kind of proof.

name: Russ Dunmore
personality: Decent, tired, deeply invested in everything being fine. Keeps the peace mostly by refusing to acknowledge there is anything to disturb it.
voice: Official and reassuring, a man reading a statement he wrote himself to feel better.

relationships.note: Their Thursday game is the last place the lake is still officially nothing. Hank has gone quiet at the table.
Gerald keeps filing reports. Russ keeps marking them 'no action required'. The file is getting thick.

secret: He drove out to the lake alone at 3 a.m. to prove to himself it was nothing, sat in the cruiser for an hour, and left without getting out of the car. He has told no one.
mood: Settled and mildly self-satisfied, a man who has found an acceptable arrangement with the geese and is prepared to defend it diplomatically.
arc: The denial arc is fully composted. He is now engaged in the ongoing low-stakes bureaucratic management of ordinary town life, which suits him. The scarf stays at the Gazette. He has decided this is intentional, which makes it true.
memory: Day 11: Stood at the water's edge without the car between him and it. Filed the letter with the Gazette. Slept.

Day 12: Walked to the dock at 7:15 a.m. without his notebook for the third morning. Left his scarf at the shore on Tuesday and has not retrieved it from the Gazette lost-and-found. Opened the station on time.
Day 13: Stood at the shore at dusk with six others. Made no report. The scarf is still at the Gazette. He is letting it be there.
Day 14: Walked to the water at dawn. It looked normal. Wrote to the Gazette about the scarf. Filed the station log: 'All quiet. Shore clear. Good morning.' Did not retrieve the scarf.
Day 15: Lost a territorial negotiation with the geese. Filed a report. Wrote to the Gazette to clarify the record on the geese and acknowledge the scarf situation. Did not retrieve the scarf.
Day 16: Filed the commons territorial agreement (north half: geese; curb and post-box: constable). Wrote to the Gazette to clarify that the scarf situation is deliberate. It is deliberate now.
backstory: Twenty years keeping a town this small means twenty years of nothing happening, which made him very good at his job and completely unprepared for the one time it didn't hold.
